output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 0.6, "R1.1": 0.2}}

Reviewed the section for internal consistency; found several breaks in the argument chain, summarized below.

1. "Portability" contradicts "Training." The Portability paragraph claims the framework "requires no labeled difficulty information" and can be deployed "without any ground-truth curation." But the Training paragraph builds the supervised corpus by running S and L offline and scoring their outputs with task-specific judges to produce labels y(x). That labeling pipeline is exactly a form of ground-truth curation (it requires reference outputs or judges per task family). Either (a) the portability claim should be narrowed — e.g., "no human difficulty annotations are required; labels are generated automatically by running S, L, and a judge" — or (b) the Section 5 deployment must explain how judges are obtained in the new domain without curation. As written, the two paragraphs cannot both be true.

2. The AUC → accuracy-gap inference is a non sequitur. The final sentence says "From this AUC we therefore conclude that ROUTELM preserves end-task accuracy to within 0.5 points of the pure-L baseline." AUC on the binary routing task measures ranking quality of phi's scores against the acceptability label y(x); it does not, by itself, pin down the end-task accuracy gap at the chosen operating threshold tau. The accuracy gap depends on (i) the threshold, (ii) the cost of false positives (cases where S is dispatched but is not actually acceptable), and (iii) the judge's calibration to the real end-task metric. A 0.5-point claim requires direct measurement on the held-out split, not a derivation from AUC. Replace "From this AUC we therefore conclude…" with an empirical statement, e.g., "Measured on the held-out split at the selected tau, end-task accuracy is within 0.5 points of the pure-L baseline."

3. The threshold-selection description is in tension with the cost claim. Routing says tau is "selected on a held-out split to enforce a target accuracy floor," but the cost analysis reports a fixed 64/36 S/L split and a 60 percent cost reduction without stating what accuracy floor was targeted. The reader cannot tell whether the 60 percent figure corresponds to the same operating point as the 0.5-point accuracy claim. State the target floor explicitly and tie the dispatch ratio, cost reduction, and accuracy gap to that single operating point.

4. Class-balancing rationale is slightly off. "Class balancing is applied because S is judged acceptable on 71 percent of prompts on average." A 71/29 split is mildly imbalanced but not severe; more importantly, balancing is a choice about training dynamics, not a logical necessity. Either justify why 71/29 motivates balancing (e.g., the minority class — "route to L" — is the costlier error to miss) or soften to "We apply class balancing during training; the base rate of acceptable S outputs is 71 percent."

5. Minor: the cost arithmetic is consistent but worth spelling out. With S at 1/20 of L and a 64/36 dispatch, expected per-token cost is 0.64·(1/20) + 0.36·1 ≈ 0.392, i.e., a ~60.8 percent reduction. Fine, but the claim implicitly assumes equal token counts per query between S and L routes; if average output lengths differ, restate accordingly.

Recommended minimal edit to the last paragraph:

"On the eight-task suite, at the tau enforcing a [X-point] accuracy floor, ROUTELM dispatches 64 percent of queries to S and 36 percent to L, reducing total inference cost by 60 percent relative to a pure-L baseline (assuming comparable per-query token counts). The router phi achieves AUC 0.89 on the binary routing task, and measured end-task accuracy on the held-out split is within 0.5 points of the pure-L baseline."

Uncertainty: I have not verified the 60.8 percent arithmetic against the paper's actual token-cost model (point 5 assumes per-token costs and equal token counts); and I cannot confirm whether Section 5 in fact resolves the portability/labeling tension (point 1).
